evals.query: I just cloned a new Rust project and I want to set up an LLM wiki to track my understanding of the codebase. The project is a web server using Axum and it has a complex middleware stack. Help me initialize a wiki in a wiki/ directory with the proper structure and create a SCHEMA.md that fits a Rust web project.
expected_behavior: Creates wiki/ with raw/ and pages/ subdirectory trees plus index.md and log.md per the bootstrap structure
Creates wiki/SCHEMA.md customized for a Rust Axum web project (domain-appropriate page-type guidance, conventions, ingest scope) rather than a generic copy
Adds the Knowledge Base - Wiki section to AGENTS.md using the template verbatim

evals.query: I have a wiki set up and I just added a new source file: wiki/raw/papers/paper-crdt-2011.md. It's a paper about Conflict-free Replicated Data Types. Please ingest this source into the wiki — create a source summary, update relevant concept pages, and create any new concept pages that are needed.
expected_behavior: Creates a source summary page in wiki/pages/sources/
Creates or updates CRDT-related concept pages using the matching templates
Adds index.md entries for every new page and appends one ingest entry to log.md

evals.query: My wiki has been growing for a while and I suspect there might be some issues. Can you run a lint pass on the wiki to check for broken links, orphan pages, missing concepts, and contradictions? Report what you find and propose fixes.
expected_behavior: Runs the six lint checks (contradictions, stale claims, orphans, term gaps, missing cross-references, research gaps) plus the AGENTS.md section check
Proposes fixes and asks confirmation before applying non-trivial ones
Appends a lint entry to wiki/log.md in the documented format

evals.query: I need to understand how authentication works in my system. Looking at my wiki, can you query the relevant pages and give me a synthesis of how authentication is implemented, what concepts are involved, and what design decisions were made?
expected_behavior: Answers by synthesizing across the relevant concept, entity, and decision pages rather than dumping excerpts
Cites at section level using the [[type-name]] §Section format
Surfaces gaps noticed and offers follow-up questions or filing the synthesis back

evals.query: After researching different database options, I've decided to use PostgreSQL with SeaORM instead of SQLite. This is a significant architectural decision. Please create a decision page documenting this choice, including the alternatives considered (SQLite, MySQL, Diesel) and the rationale.
expected_behavior: Creates wiki/pages/decisions/decision-<name>.md following the decision template (status, date, context, decision, rationale, consequences, related)
Updates index.md and appends to log.md

evals.query: [Agent has a subagent-spawning tool available] My wiki covers caching, session storage, and rate limiting across many pages. How do these three subsystems interact, and where do their designs conflict?
expected_behavior: Makes ONE subagent call whose spawn prompt instructs the subagent to first read references/agents/librarian.md and then answer the question verbatim
Does NOT read the librarian role file or any wiki page into the main thread
Relays the subagent's synthesized answer with [[type-name]] §Section citations and surfaces gaps it noticed
Offers to file the synthesis back, and does that filing inline (no scribe spawn for a single page)

evals.query: [Agent has a subagent-spawning tool available; wiki/index.md has NOT been read this session] What port does the dev server run on? It's documented in the wiki.
expected_behavior: Delegates as one librarian call (index not resident, so delegation is cheaper than pulling index + pages into the main thread) OR answers inline only if index.md is already in context and the fact is in 1-2 known pages
If delegating: the spawn prompt tells the subagent to read the role file itself — the main agent does not read references/agents/librarian.md
Total main-thread wiki footprint stays at roughly one tool call plus a terse answer

evals.query: [Agent has a subagent-spawning tool available] Run a lint pass on the wiki and apply the fixes I approve.
expected_behavior: Runs the audit as ONE librarian subagent call (task = audit) covering all six checks in the subagent's thread; no parallel per-check fan-out unless the user asked for speed
Checks the AGENTS.md wiki section in the main thread
Applies approved multi-page fixes through a SINGLE scribe subagent call (never two scribes in parallel) that appends the lint report to wiki/log.md
Never reads role files into the main thread — spawn prompts tell subagents to read their own role file

evals.query: [Agent has a subagent-spawning tool available with web access] Ingest this source into the wiki: https://example.com/blog/event-sourcing-patterns
expected_behavior: Makes three subagent calls in sequence, never reading the source, wiki files, or role files in the main thread
Call 1 (researcher): fetches the URL and writes a provenance-stamped raw note under wiki/raw/articles/, touching nothing under wiki/pages/, index.md, or log.md
Call 2 (librarian, source analysis): returns takeaways and a proposed page plan, which the main agent discusses with the user
Call 3 (scribe): executes the approved plan from a standalone brief that includes the source path and templates directory, updating index.md and log.md
Each spawn prompt instructs the subagent to read its own role file first

evals.query: [Agent has NO subagent-spawning capability] My wiki covers caching, session storage, and rate limiting across many pages. How do these three subsystems interact?
expected_behavior: Performs the synthesis workflow directly: reads index.md, reads the relevant pages, answers with [[type-name]] §Section citations
Makes no attempt to spawn subagents
Output quality is indistinguishable from the delegated path
